coredata.dc:title: Efficient solar to acetate conversion in microbial electrosynthesis with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode coordination under visible light

coredata.dc:description: Photo-assisted microbial electrosynthesis (MES) is a novel carbon-neutral approach to convert CO2 into chemicals. The low separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs affects the performance of MES. In this study, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode were designed by co-modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and FeOOH layers on BiVO4 and g-C3N4 to enhance acetate production in photo-assisted MES. CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode enhances the oxygen evolution reaction rate and reduces the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode with fast photoelectron transfer and good biocompatibility increases direct or indirect via H2 electron transfer rate to electroautotrophic microorganisms. At −0.9 V (vs Ag/AgCl), the acetate yield of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under visible light illumination is 2.6 times that of the control, and achieves acetate yield of 0.51 g/L/d with solar-to-acetate conversion efficiency of 1.78 % and the current of 45 mA within 14 days. This result provides a feasible strategy to construct photocatalysts in MES for efficient conversion of solar to chemicals.

head.ce:title: Efficient solar to acetate conversion in microbial electrosynthesis with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode coordination under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-assisted microbial electrosynthesis (MES) is a novel carbon-neutral approach to convert CO2 into chemicals. The low separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs affects the performance of MES. In this study, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode were designed by co-modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and FeOOH layers on BiVO4 and g-C3N4 to enhance acetate production in photo-assisted MES. CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode enhances the oxygen evolution reaction rate and reduces the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode with fast photoelectron transfer and good biocompatibility increases direct or indirect via H2 electron transfer rate to electroautotrophic microorganisms. At −0.9 V (vs Ag/AgCl), the acetate yield of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under visible light illumination is 2.6 times that of the control, and achieves acetate yield of 0.51 g/L/d with solar-to-acetate conversion efficiency of 1.78 % and the current of 45 mA within 14 days. This result provides a feasible strategy to construct photocatalysts in MES for efficient conversion of solar to chemicals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rising concentration of CO2 in the atmosphere has seriously affected the global environment and climate [1], and carbon neutrality has become one of the development goals of many countries [2,3]. One of the most effective ways is to use CO2 and renewable energy for biological production [4]. Microbial electrosynthesis (MES) offers a bioelectrochemical approach based on using electricity from renewable energy sources to provide the reducing equivalents for microbially catalyzed CO2 reduction into valuable chemicals [5]. Thus, MES has attracted considerable attention from researchers regarding CO2 fixation and energy storage [6,7].

In MES, microorganisms can either directly receive electrons or indirectly obtain electrons from hydrogen to reduce carbon dioxide [8]. However, the low electron transfer rate between the electrode and microorganisms limits the efficiency of MES. The use of semiconductor photocatalyst materials to enhance the electron transfer rate is an effective strategy for improving the performance of MES [9]. In photo-assisted MES system, light irradiation can excite the semiconductor material to produce photogenerated electron-holes pairs. Photogenerated holes are refilled by the electrons traveling from the anode provide additional driving force, while photogenerated-electrons are transferred to electrotrophs for supply to reduce CO2 to acetate. However, the photo-assisted MES still faces problems such as the slow separation rate of photogenerated electron holes and poor biocompatibility.

Si nanowire and TiO2 are first reported as photoanode and photocathode [10] of MES, respectively. Unlike TiO2, which can only be excited in ultraviolet light, g-C3N4 can be excited in visible light [11], further, the conduction band (CB) edge of g-C3N4 was more negative than that of a H+/H2 redox pair, it is favorable for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), providing microorganisms with reducing power to reduce CO2, thus some g-C3N4-based heterojunction photocatalyst is used as the photocathode of MES, such as WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 [12], Ag3PO4/g-C3N4 [13], α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [14], and CuO/g-C3N4 [15], MnFe2O4/g-C3N4 [16] and ZIF-67/g-C3N4 [17]. However, good biocompatibility, fast electron transfer capability and efficient charge carriers separation are the directions that g-C3N4-based heterojunction photocatalyst need to improve. In addition, the photoanode can also reduce the overpotential of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and transfer photogenerated electrons from anode to the cathode, thereby improving the performance of MES. Compared with the photocathode on MES, there are few research on the photoanode in MES [10,17,18]. BiVO4 is a visible light semiconductor photocatalyst, which has a relatively narrow band gap about 2.4 eV, and a valence band (VB) edge position of BiVO4 is conducive to the oxidation half reaction of water decomposition [19]. The enhanced electron transfer capacity of the anode will also improve the overall electron supply of MES, which will be conducive to the cathode reduction rate of CO2 to chemicals.

Iron oxyhydroxide (FeOOH), as a transition metal oxide semiconductor, is highly available in soils, water, and rocks on the Earth’s surface and FeOOH is widely used in electrode material, biosensor, and lithium-ion battery [20,21]. Due to the sufficient oxygen vacancies (Ov) [22] in FeOOH, FeOOH catalysts are introduced as photocatalysts for HER and OER, such as FeOOH/NiOOH/BiVO4 [23], FeOOH/g-C3N4 [24] and FeOOH/CdS [25]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), well-known as carbon-based nanomaterial, have replaced various semiconductor quantum dots in recent years, since they are nontoxic, earth abundant, good biocompatibility and excellent photostability [26]. Furthermore, the π-conjugated structure of CQDs can serve as an electron acceptor and donor by transfer of photogenerated electrons [27]. CQDs can also improve the visible light response [28]. Thus, CQDs have been widely utilized to modified various semi-conductor, such as g-C3N4 [29], Fe3O4 [30], Bi2WO6 [31] . Thus, CQDs/FeOOH nanocomposite modification photoelectrode (BiVO4 and g-C3N4) is expected to construct an intermediate hole and electron transport layer for enhancing the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole, and accelerating the electron transfer rate in the photo-assisted MES. Moreover, c-type cytochromes on the cell membrane have a high binding affinity to FeOOH [32], CQDs/FeOOH modification on g-C3N4 is conducive to improving the biocompatibility of the photoelectrode. Besides, for photo-assisted MES, the simultaneous use of the photocathode and the photoanode on MES is more beneficial for improving the efficiency of light harvesting [17]. Thus, the use of the CQDs/FeOOH nanocomposite to simultaneous develop efficient photocathode and photoanode will be conducive to enhance the solar conversion efficiencies, improve the performance of MES and simplify the modification process of the photoelectrode.

Therefore, the MES with CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/ g-C3N4 photocathode was used for the CO2 reduction to acetate in the current study. The simultaneous enhanced photoelectrodes performance resulted in high acetate production in MES. The enhancement mechanism of photo-assisted MES with CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/ g-C3N4 photocathode was discussed in detail based on material structure characterization, optical properties, electrochemical properties and MES experiments. At last, this study provided a simple and sustainable strategy to promote photo-assisted MES by simultaneous modification photocathode and photoanode.

BiVO4 photoanode (RuO2-IrO2/Ti as material base, 2.5 cm × 7 cm) was prepared via electrochemical deposition method as previously reported [17]. First, 6.64 g of KI and 1.94 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were added to 100 mL of deionized water. Then, 40 mL of 0.23 mol/L 1, 4-benzoquinone ethanol solution was added and the pH of solution was adjusted to 3.5 as electrolyte. RuO2-IrO2/Ti used as the anode and cathode respectively. The BiOI electrode was obtained by electrochemical deposition method (0.5 V, 400 s) and cleaned with deionized water. Then, vanadyl acetylacetonate (0.2 M) dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide was brushed homogeneously onto the BiOI electrode and kept in air condition for 1 h at 420 ℃. After natural cooling, the BiVO4 photoanode was successfully prepared. Moreover, the electrodeposition of FeOOH on BiVO4 photoanode was constructed in a 0.1 M FeSO4 solution with the voltage of 1.2 V for 1 min [33]. CQDs was prepared by electrochemical stripping [34]. The two graphite rods (the raw materials for CQDs) used as the anode and cathode respectively, and the ultrapure water served as the electrolyte. A direct current power (36 V) was applied to the two electrodes for 120 h. A dark-yellow CQDs solution from anode corrosion was obtained. Further, the solution was filtered, centrifuged and dried at 60 °C to obtain CQDs. Nafion solution was used as binder to mix with CQDs (20 mg). The FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was immersed in the above CQDs solution and shaken for 5 h, and dried to prepare the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode. The same method was used to prepare CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode.

g-C3N4 was synthesized by melamine decomposition as previously reported [15]. Furthermore, g-C3N4 (250 mg) mixed with isopropyl and Nafion solution. Carbon felt (CF) (50 mm × 50 mm × 5 mm, length × width × thickness) was used as the photocathode base. The CF was immersed into the above g-C3N4 solution and stirred for 5 h and prepared the g-C3N4 photocathode after drying. The FeOOH modification of g-C3N4 photocathode was still the same electrochemical deposition method as the photoanode. Furthermore, the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was still constructed by bonding CQDs to the FeOOH/g-C3N4 through Nafion solution. The same method was used to prepare CQDs/g-C3N4 photocathode.

The H-type reactor consisted of two separate glass chambers with each internal effective volume of 280 mL (Fig. S1a) [35]. The Plate-type reactor (Fig. S1b) made from two cubic plexiglass (effective volume of 170 mL) was used as previously reported [17]. The cation exchange membrane (CEM) (Nafion 117, DuPont Co., USA) divided the reactor into a cathodic chamber and an anodic chamber. The prepared photocathode and photoanode were used and Ag/AgCl reference electrode was placed near the cathode. The composition of anolyte and catholyte was as previously reported [17]. The cathode chamber was inoculated with suspensions from long-run MES. The potential was controlled using a cell test system (Neware, Shenzhen, China) and the light intensity of 36.5 kLux (100 W iodine tungsten lamp) was used. The 100% CO2 was sparged at 3 mL/min in cathode chamber. The temperature was maintained at 30 ± 2 °C in all parallel experimental groups. The products were regularly determined in batch MES reactors.

The structure and crystal pattern were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental compositions were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, UlVAC-PHI, Japan). SEM (JSM-5900, Japan) and TEM (JEOL, Japan) were used to examine the surface structure of the sample. The optical properties of samples were analyzed by UV−vis DRS (Hitachi U-3900) and photoluminescence (PL, F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (Bruker A300 spectrometer, Germany) was applied for investigation of the existence of Ov.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode system (Details in the supporting information). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried at scan rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with an amplitude of 5 mV between 0.01 ∼ 100 kHz, where Nyquist plots was acquired from Zsimpwin software. Furthermore, Cyclic voltammetry (CV) with different scanning rates via Lavrion's equation [36] were tested to characterize apparent electron transfer rate constants (Kapp).

Hydrogen concentration was measured using a gas chromatograph (GC-2010 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). Acetate concentration was determined using HPLC (Agilent Technologies 1260) (Details in the supporting information). 5 g/L of KI as hole scavenger was used to investigate the effect of holes on MES. The energy conversion efficiency of the solar-driven CO2 reduction was calculated according to following equation:
Energy conversion efficiency ( 100 % )
=
η
V
× I
A·
cm
2

− 2

Here I (A∙cm−2) was the current density of the electrode, 0.05 W∙ cm2 was the light intensity of simulated sunlight, and η (V) was the thermodynamic potential to reduce CO2 into acetate accordance with the Nernst equation.

The CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 anode was prepared on RuO2-IrO2/Ti ( Fig. 1a). The TEM image (Fig.S2) showed that the size of the CQDs was 3–6 nm and the crystal lattice spacing around 0.32 nm correspond to the (002) lattice planes of graphitic carbon [37]. CQDs had an absorption value under UV/Vis from 228 nm (Fig. S2b) and the PL emission spectra of CQDs (Fig. S2c) showed its optical properties [34]. These results proved that CQDs had been synthesized successfully. Characteristic peaks of IrO2, RuO2, Ti and BiVO4 [17] can be observed at the XRD lines of BiVO4 sample (JCPDS#175–1866) (Fig. 1b). This result indicated that BiOV4 had been loaded onto the substrate (Titanium meshes with iridium and ruthenium coatings). In addition, no significant additional diffraction peaks were found on FeOOH/BiVO4 (JCPDS#81–0462) and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 (JCPDS#89–8494), it may be due to the low FeOOH and CQDs loading amount on photoanode. The XPS spectra of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 (Fig. 1c) contained typical elements Bi, Fe, C, V, O, implied that FeOOH and CQDs had been modified successfully on the surface of BiVO4. For the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1d), peak at 529.8 eV and 531.5 eV was corresponded to lattice oxygen and Ov in oxygen species [38], respectively. The BiVO4 had the lattice oxygen, with the addition of FeOOH, the Ov was 45.6 % of the total oxygen species in FeOOH/BiVO4. Further, the Ov increased to 53.8 % in CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4. The EPR result (Fig.S6c) also proved that the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 contained the highest Ov. These results indicated that the introduction of CODS and FeOOH can increase the proportion of Ov in the catalyst [21,39] and most of the Ov came from FeOOH's contribution. From the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1e), C-O (286.1 eV) and C= O (288.1 eV) groups were only found in the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, suggesting the successful introduction of CQDs into the catalyst [40]. The Bi 4 f (Fig.S3a) showed the binding energies (BE) of Bi 4f7/2(158.8 eV) and Bi 4f5/2(164.1 eV), while the V 2p (Fig.S3b) showed the BE of V 2p3/2 (516.5 eV) and V 2p1/2 (524.2 eV), corresponding to Bi3+ and V5+ in BiVO4. The Fe 2p peak (Fig.S3c) was obtained in CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 and FeOOH/BiVO4. It can be seen from the SEM image (Fig.S4a) that synthesized BiVO4 showed a tube-like morphology. The smaller-sized FeOOH (Fig.S4b) was uniformly dispersed on the surface of BiVO4 and acted as a "bridge" for hole transfer. CQD (Fig. 1f) was embedded in the pores of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4, thereby forming a high-speed channel for electron transport. The HRTEM image of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 was further observed (Fig. 1g). The lattice spacing of 0.26 nm and 0.23 nm corresponded to the (200) and (211) of the BiVO4 [41]. A lattice spacing of 0.32 nm attributed to the (002) plane of graphite carbon, implying that CQDs were obtained. A lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, consistent with the (111) plane of FeOOH. The HRTEM image confirmed the successful incorporation of CQDs and FeOOH on BiVO4.

The photocurrent response ( Fig. 2a) is used to evaluate the separation effect of photogenerated electron holes [42]. After combination with FeOOH or CQDs, the photocurrent of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 increased. The peak photocurrent of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 was higher than that of BiVO4. Furthermore, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 had the highest photocurrent among all photoanode. These results implied that CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 had the best photoelectrocatalytic performance. The results of photocurrent responses were consistent with the LSV (Fig. 2b). The current density of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 rose most rapidly with the increase of potential, and the current density of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 were higher than that of BiVO4. The Tafel slope (Fig. 2c) from LSV calculation of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 was lowest (0.12 V dec−1), followed by FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4. BiVO4 had the highest Tafel slope (0.18 V dec−1), indicating that the CQDs/FeOOH coating could help improve OER reaction and enhance oxygen evolution kinetic. The resistance of photocathode was analyzed via EIS (Fig. 2d) and the corresponding values were obtained using ZSimpWin software. The internal resistance (Rint) was composed of anodic charge transfer resistances (Rp2), cathodic charge transfer resistances (Rp1) and solution (ohmic) resistances (Rs) in Table 1. The variation in Rint mostly originated from Rp2. Rs and Rp1 did not differ significantly across all groups due to the same electrolyte composition and cathode. Compared with BiVO4 (15.9 Ω), FeOOH/BiVO4 decreased the Rint (13.3 Ω), while CQDs/BiVO4 increased the Rint (17.1 Ω). With further combination of CQDs on FeOOH/BiVO4, the Rint of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 reached 16.7 Ω. This reason might be that there were oxygen-containing groups on the CQDs, resulting in a decrease in its electron conductivity. The combination of CQDs/FeOOH on BiVO4 did not result in significant differences in Rint.

The PL spectra (Fig. 2e) was further used to investigate the photogenerated hole−electron pairs separation rate. BiVO4 had the highest luminescence intensity, with the introduction of FeOOH on BiVO4, the luminescence intensity decreased, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 obtained the lowest luminescence intensity, indicating that the modification of CQDs/FeOOH on BiVO4 had the slowest photogenic electron hole recombination rate. Based on UV–Vis DRS analysis test (Fig. 2f), the introduction of FeOOH, the absorption band of FeOOH/BiVO4 increased to 562 nm, compared to the BiVO4 (507 nm). The introduction of CQDs increased partial light harvesting in visible regions. The combination of CQDs on FeOOH/BiVO4, the obvious red shift was obtained (522 nm) and significantly increased light harvesting in the ultraviolet and visible regions. This result showed that the CQDs/FeOOH coating can improve the light harvesting ability. In accordance with the Tauc plot, the bandgap of the BiVO4 was 2.47 eV (Fig.S5a). After combination of FeOOH or CQDs, the bandgap of FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 decreased to 2.24 eV and 2.23 eV, this was consistent with the band gaps of BiVO4 (2.41) and CQD/BiVO4 (2.28) [28]. Furthermore, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 can obtain the lowest bandgap (1.96 eV), indicating that loading CQDs/FeOOH on BiVO4 can obtain wide absorption range of light, which is characteristic of surface-defect site derived from CQDs as reported [28]. Compared to TiO2 /FTO (3.2 eV) [29] and BiVO4/Mo/FTO (2.6 eV) [18] as photoanodes in MES, CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 enhanced the absorption intensity of visible light and expanded the absorption range of light, which was beneficial to harvest more solar for the conversion of photogenerated electron-holes in MES. The positive slopes in the Mott−Schottky curves (Fig.S5b) of the catalysts confirmed that the combination of FeOOH or CQDs/FeOOH did not change the BiVO4 semiconductor n-type [43]. It can be seen from the above results that the modification of CQDs/FeOOH is beneficial to enlarge the light absorption band and improve the OER performance and photoelectron hole separation rate.

The same strategy to prepare the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode was illustrated in Fig. 3a. The XRD pattern of g-C3N4 (JCPDS#87–1526) in Fig. 3b showed two characteristic peaks at 13.2 and 27.7° attributed to the (100) plane of triazine and the (002) plane of conjugated aromatic systems, respectively. With FeOOH and CQDs/FeOOH modification, the diffraction peaks of photocathode were unchanged, but the intensity gradually decreased, owing to their shield effect to X-ray. The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 3c) for CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 showed the presence of elements C, N, Fe and O, implying the binding of CQDs/FeOOH to g-C3N4. For the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 3d), the O 1 s spectra showed a peak at 532.06 eV on pure g-C3N4 owing to the adsorbed H2O, and an additional peak at 531.5 eV was assigned to Ov. The Ov was 46.8 % of the oxygen species in FeOOH/g-C3N4 and increased to 50.3 % in CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4. The highest response signal peak can be observed on CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 in EPR (Fig.S6d). These results showed that the introduction of CODS and FeOOH can also increase the proportion of Ov in the photocathode. As shown in Fig. 3e, three C1s peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.2 eV represent C-C/ CC, C-OH, and N-CN [44]. An obvious shift of C 1 s peak at 288.4 eV in the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 compared with that of g-C3N4 (288.2 eV), indicating the chemical interaction between CQDs/FeOOH and g-C3N4. Furthermore, the peak intensity of C−OH (286.2 eV) increased after CQDs modification. The N 1 s spectra (Fig.S6a) showed typical N status exists in the catalyst as pyridinic (398.8 eV), pyrrolic (400.2 eV) and π-excitations (404.3 eV) [45], respectively. Two main peaks at 710.9 eV and 725.0 eV were corresponding to the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the formation of FeOOH on photocathode (Fig.S6b). As shown in the SEM image, g-C3N4 exhibited a block-like structure (Fig.S7a), the FeOOH (Fig.S7b) and CQDs (Fig. 3f) nanolayers had been attached to the surface of g-C3N4. The HRTEM image of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was further observed (Fig. 3g). The lattice spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.67 nm corresponded to the (002) and (100) of the g-C3N4 [46,47]. CQDs (0.32 nm) and FeOOH (0.24 nm) were anchored on the surface of g-C3N4, indicating that the CQDs/FeOOH were successfully hybridized on the g-C3N4. These results showed that the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was successfully synthesized.

The photocurrent intensity of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was significantly higher than others ( Fig. 4a), indicating CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 had the highest fastest separation rate of photogenerated carriers. Furthermore, g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 had the lowest current density in LSV (Fig. 4b), the FeOOH modification on g-C3N4 enhanced the current density, CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 had the highest current density in LSV (Fig. 4b). The Tafel curve based on LSV calculation was estimated to hydrogen evolution properties of the photocathode (Fig. 4c). CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 had the lowest Tafel slope (0.07 V dec−1), these results showed that CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 has the highest hydrogen evolution performance in all photocathode. The EIS diagram of as prepared photocathode was shown in Fig. 4d, the Rint of g-C3N4 was 20.6 Ω ( Table 2), the modification of FeOOH is conducive to the reduction of Rint (17.4 Ω), while the modification of CQDs will increase the Rint (22.5Ω). The modification of CQDs/FeOOH composite made the Rint (20.7 Ω) close to that of g-C3N4. It shows that the modification of CQDs/FeOOH on g-C3N4 has little effect on the internal resistance. From UV–Vis DRS (Fig. 4e), the absorption edge of g-C3N4 was approximately at 443 nm, CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 broadened the light response range and showed the strongest absorption in the visible and infrared region.

Furthermore, the bandgap (Fig.S8a) of g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4, FeOOH/g-C3N4 and CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was 2.78 eV, 2.23 eV, 2.3 eV and 2.13 eV, respectively. This also proves that the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 can obtain the widest light absorption. The positive slope of photocathode from Mott−Schottky curves (Fig.S8b) demonstrated they were n-type semiconductors. From PL spectra (Fig. 4f), the intensity of emission peak for CQDs/g-C3N4 and FeOOH/g-C3N4 was lower than that of g-C3N4. CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 had the lowest emission intensity, meaning the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. These results indicated that the modification of CQDs/FeOOH on g-C3N4 enhanced light absorption capacity and HER performance, improved the charge recombination rate.

The FeOOH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 can improve the current value of MES ( Fig. 5a–c) at −0.9 V (versus Ag/AgCl), while the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 can obtain the highest current value. Corresponding, the acetate concentration of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 (2.65 ± 0.04 g/L) was higher than FeOOH/BiVO4 (1.88 ± 0.03 g/L) and CQDs/BiVO4 (1.22 ± 0.05 g/L). The highest acetate production rate of CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 (0.19 g/L/ d) was 2.3 times than that of BiVO4 (0.08 g/L/d). H2 production in CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 was also highest (0.09 m3/m3/d). The addition of CQDs/FeOOH enhanced the reaction rate of OER and the production of photogenerated electrons, allowing more electrons to be transferred to the cathode, it helped microbes receive electrons to fix CO2.

As RuO2-IrO2/Ti has long working life, strong corrosion resistance and high electrocatalytic activity, it is often used as anode in MES [48–50]. Thus, the effect of photocathode on the performance of MES with the RuO2-IrO2/Ti anode was further studied at −0.9 V (Fig. 5d–f). CQDs/FeOOH modified g-C3N4 can still obtain the highest reaction current, followed by FeOOH/g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4. CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 obtained the highest acetate concentration (3.64 ± 0.24 g/L) and acetate yield (0.26 g/L/d), while the g-C3N4 can only obtain 0.12 g/L/d of acetate yield.

The hydrogen yield of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 reached to 0.11 m3/m3/d, which was higher than that of FeOOH/g-C3N4 (0.06 m3/m3/d) and CQDs/g-C3N4 (0.05 m3/m3/d), the hydrogen yield of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 was 3.6 times than that of the g-C3N4 (0.03 m3/m3/d). These results indicated that the modification of CQDs/FeOOH nanocomposite on g-C3N4 was favorable to produce photogenic electrons in the cathode and these electrons can combine with protons to produce more hydrogen [14], thus providing more reducing power for microorganisms to fix CO2.

Both CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode alone can enhance the performance of MES, and further synergies of the above modified photoelectrodes were carried out in Plate-type reactor for reducing the internal resistance (Fig.S9). The average current of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was about 15 mA without light (Fig. 5g). When CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 or CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 under illumination, the average current of MES elevated to 22 mA and 25 mA, respectively. When the anode and cathode were illuminated at the same time, the current further increased to 45 mA and kept relatively stable until the end of the experiment. Furthermore, the acetate yield of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under dark was 0.19 g/L/d (Fig. 5h). When the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 or CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 alone was exposed to light, the acetate yield increased to about 0.29 g/L/d and 0.26 g/L/d. The MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under light had the highest acetate yield (0.51 g/L/d), which was 2.7 times that of same MES without illumination. This result proved that the excitation of the photoelectrode from light significantly improved the performance of MES. When the unmodified BiVO4 and g-C3N4 were used as the photoanode and photocathode (Fig. S10), respectively. The corresponding current of MES was average 15 mA, and the acetate yield was only 0.2 g/L/d. With the introduction of CQDs/FeOOH on BiVO4 and g-C3N4, the yield of acetate yield in MES increased 2.6-fold. The acetate yield of MES in Plate-type reactor was higher than that of MES with same photoelectrodes in H-type reactor (0.37 g/L/d) (Fig.S11), the corresponding current and hydrogen production in Plate-type reactor was also higher than that of H-type reactor. These results also proved that P-type reactor with shorter spacing was more favorable to reflect the role of dual photoelectrode on MES performance. There is also a significant increase in hydrogen production (0.34 m3/m3/d) with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under light, while the hydrogen production was only 0.03 m3/m3/d with same photoelectrode under dark (Fig. 5i). The result showed that under the condition of illumination, the synergistic enhancement of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode can produce more hydrogen, which leads to the increase of acetate yield in MES.

CV curves with different scanning speed can be used to evaluate electron transfer rate between electrodes interface and microorganisms [51,52]. Figs. 6a-6d showed CV curves of the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode under different light conditions. The redox peak value of CV curve increased with the scanning rate, indicating that the process is controlled by kinetics, the corresponding Kapp value was calculated based on CV curve (Figs. 6e-6h). In the absence of light, Kapp value of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode was only 4.6 s−1. When the photoanode or photocathode was illuminated alone, its Kapp value increased to 5.4 s−1 and 5.2 s−1. The Kapp value was the highest (5.9 s−1) under the condition that both photoelectrodes were illuminated. This result shows that the synergistic effect of CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode can further improve the electron transfer rate between the microbe and the electrode under light. In addition, Kapp values in Plate-type reactor were higher than those in H-type reactors under the same conditions (Fig.S12). It also shows again that the Plate-type reactor is more conducive to reflect the function of photocatalytic electrodes and improve the electron transfer between microorganisms and electrodes.

KI, as a scavenge agent for holes [53], was used to evaluate the effect of photocathode generated holes on the performance of MES. In MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode at −0.9 V, the addition of KI (Fig.S13) decreased the reaction average current from 45 mA to 16 mA, and the corresponding acetate yield from 0.51 g/L/d to 0.18 g/L/d. With the addition of KI, photogenerated holes no longer combined with more electrons from the anode, and the supply of microbial reducing power was reduced, resulting in low acetate production yield. This result showed that photogenerated holes in cathode provided additional driving force to accelerate the transfer of electrons for the bioconversion of carbon dioxide.

Furthermore, the performance of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode at low applied potential (absolute value from −0.9 V to −0.6 V) was further investigated ( Fig. 7a-c). When the potential (absolute value) was lower than −0.9 V, the current, acetate production and hydrogen production will be significantly decreased with the potential decline. At −0.6 V, the current of the MES was 1.5 mA, acetate yield reached 0.07 g/L/d, hydrogen production is only (0.008 m3/m3/d). At −0.6 V, the hydrogen evolution reaction is difficult to carry out [54] and is dominated by direct electron transfer, resulting in a low acetate yield. However, these direct electron transfers mostly appear on pure bacteria, such as Sporomusa ovata [5], Clostridium ljungdahlii [55], while mixed culture are difficult to produce acetate at low potential. But mixed culture has stronger performance in tolerating environmental pressure and other disturbances, thus mixed culture are capable of efficiently producing acetate in complex environments from hydrogen-mediated electron transfer. This showed that CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode helped to reduce the overpotential of OER at low potentials, and used holes in photocathode to induce photoanode electrons to come quickly, and supplied reducing power to microorganisms at low potentials. At high potential, more photogenerated electrons converted protons into hydrogen, allowing microorganisms to gain more reducing power via hydrogen, combined with the driving force of photogenic holes, resulting in a high acetate yield.

The photocatalytic mechanism of MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode ( Fig. 8) was proposed from the above results analysis. When visible light radiated on the CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode, CQD was responsible for the transfer of photogenerated electrons, the heterojunction formed by CQDs/BiVO4 [56] allowed the photogenerated e− transfer from CB of CQDs to CB of BiVO4 and further transferred to the photocathode via external circuit, while the photogenerated holes from VB of BiVO4 transferred to VB of CQDs and would be utilized to oxidize water for oxygen production. In this process, FeOOH used as the intermediate hole transport layer to enhance charge separation, because rich Ov could help the hole trapping and migration [33]. Furthermore, the introduction of Ov from CQDs and FeOOH enhanced the OER reaction rate and the CQDs increased the light harvesting range of the BiVO4, allowing the photoanode to produce more electrons and holes. Therefore, CQDs/FeOOH nanocomposites not only enhanced the light absorption and photogenerated electron holes separation but also accelerated the OER kinetics in photoanode.

When visible light radiated on the CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode, FeOOH/g-C3N4 is a Z-scheme heterojunction structure [24]. The photogenerated electrons transferred from the CB of FeOOH to the VB of g-C3N4, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole. Furthermore, CQDs had the excellent electron storage ability and can be used as interfacial electron bridges, the corresponding photogenerated electrons transfer rate can be enhanced and restrained the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. The photogenerated holes from VB of FeOOH bound electrons from the anode, induced more electrons from the anode to cathode. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 transferred to microorganisms through direct electron transfer and indirect via H2. The introduction of CQDs and FeOOH with fast photoelectron transfer enhanced the HER reaction rate, increased the light harvesting range of the g-C3N4, accelerating ETR between the photocathode and the microorganism. In addition, exoelectrogenic bacteria can transfer electrons with electrode by electron transport proteins, such as c-type cytochromes, which have a high binding affinity to FeOOH [32]. Therefore, the biocompatibility of the photocathode was improved.

When both CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode radiated under visible light, the cathode received more electrons as the OER kinetics accelerated from the use of photoanode, the electron transfer rate of the whole MES system can be accelerated. Furthermore, the current of the external circuit was enhanced, and the rate of HER and ETR were significantly increased, giving microbes more electron donors to reduce CO2. This is beneficial for obtaining higher acetate yield in the MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode.

The MES with CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode and CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode accumulated 7.12 g/L acetate (0.51 g/L/d acetate yield) and the corresponding solar-to-acetate conversion efficiency reached 1.78 % within 14 days. This result exceeded that of BiVO4 photoanode and ZIF-67/g-C3N4 photocathode (0.46 g/L/d, 1.52 %) [17], BiVO4/Mo photoanode (0.055 g/L/d, 0.97 %) [18], and RGO-based SiO2-TiO2 photocathode (0.19 g/L/d) [57], all of which used mixed culture as biocatalysts (Table S1). For Serratia marcescens as biocatalyst for conversion of bicarbonate to acetate (Table S1), this result was also higher than WO3/MoO3/g-C3N4 photocathode (0.19 g/L/d, 0.37 %) [12], equal to urea-treated MnFe2O4/g-C3N4 photocathode (0.51 g/l/d, 1.02 %) [16], and lower than a pyrrolic nitrogen enriched Ag3PO4/g-C3N4 photocathode (0.57 g/l/d, 1.15 %) [58], but its potential value in MES with Serratia marcescens is −1.3 V vs Ag/AgCl, which was higher than the potential value (-0.9 V vs Ag/AgCl) in this study. Thus, efficient matching between CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode resulted in the highest solar-to-acetate conversion efficiency for CO2 reduction in our study. A simple method is provided for simultaneously improving the photoelectrocatalysis of the cathode and anode to enhance the performance of MES in this study. In the future, the electrode structure can be further optimized. For instance, 3D electrodes can be adopted to enhance the loading of photocatalysts and absorption of light, thereby improving the amplification performance of MES.

In this study, the MES performance with CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode and CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 photocathode was dramatically enhanced. CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 improved the OER rate from the introduction of Ov. CQDs/FeOOH/g-C3N4 increased HER ability and apparent electron transfer rate constants. The correspond acetate yield was 0.51 g/L/d and solar-to-acetate conversion efficiency reached 1.78 % within 14 days. This study demonstrates a simply and feasible strategy for design and synthesis of highly efficient photoelectrodes for conversion of energy to chemicals.
